brief_justification: Agent answers 'Yes' but correct answer is 'No' — wrong empirically, though the causal logic is internally coherent; Agent goes one level deep by distinguishing joint causation from overdetermination and explaining why both logins were necessary; No self-monitoring — agent never questions whether its causal theory accurately predicts empirical human responses, nor considers that its intuition may diverge from actual survey data; No verification attempted — agent makes no effort to sanity-check its prediction against known psychology of causal attribution; Agent states 'a typical person would answer Yes' with full confidence, no acknowledgment that this is an empirical claim with uncertainty; Agent mentions overdetermination as a contrasting case but only to reinforce its Yes answer, never genuinely evaluates whether 'No' is the response and why; The reasoning chain (joint necessity → genuine cause → Yes) is traceable step by step, though the empirical premise about typical responses is never sourced or justified.
